data.title: Workday: A Competitive Analysis With Oracle And SAP
data.description:
Investment thesis

Workday, Inc. (WDAY), is one of three leading competitors providing HCM (human capital management) and ERP (enterprise resource planning). Along with SAP SE (SAP) and Oracle Corporation (ORCL), it helps other firms do full-cycle human resource management and unify core business functions.

In this analysis, we compare the three companies across multiple financial metrics and conclude with a Buy rating for Workday. But, we also highlight the growth potential of one of the other two.

About Workday

The company offers enterprise cloud solutions powered by artificial intelligence [AI] to help companies "maintain accounting information; manage financial processes, such as payables and receivables; identify real-time financial, operational, and management insights; perform financial consolidation; reduce time-to-close; promote internal control and auditability; and achieve consistency across finance operations." (Source: Seeking Alpha).

SAP SE and Oracle Corporation provide similar but not identical offerings to their customers. For example, all three offer HCM and ERP services, but with different emphases.

The payroll consultancy firm Leppington Group offered this summary of the three firms:

"Oracle is ideal for large, global enterprises with complex payroll needs. SAP SuccessFactors is well-suited for organisations already using SAP ERP systems and requiring strong global capabilities. Workday is best for medium to large enterprises looking for a modern, user-friendly, and scalable solution."

And under the headline "Which Software Should You Buy in 2025", an article in AppCloneScript summarizes,

"Select Workday HCM if you want a holistic, intuitive, and contemporary HR software with inbuilt analytics and fast delivery. It's the preferred solution for customers in search of a proven technology foundation for the digital transformation of their business, as well as HCM professionals in organizations of any size who need to be part of a connected HCM experience.

"Go with SAP SuccessFactors if you're a large company with worldwide complexity, and especially if you're already invested in the SAP ecosystem. Its global payroll service and learning management are top of the market.

"Opt for Oracle HCM Cloud if you want a high level of configurability as well as robust analytics and integration with other Oracle apps. It applies to complicated business structures and hybrid clouds."

For investors who want exposure to HCM and/or ERP stocks, these three offer different approaches. Is one a better investment than the others? That is a question we will try to answer by analyzing their fundamentals.

Revenue, net income, and earnings

The following five-year tables show average year-over-year and forward growth rates for revenue and diluted EPS (from the growth tables at Seeking Alpha):

Workday's diluted EPS growth over the past five years is unavailable because it had a loss in 2023. That was followed by an unusually large gain in 2024.

What comes through is that Workday is a tech growth stock, while Oracle and SAP appear to be mature companies with only modest gains expected in coming years. That's reinforced by share repurchases and the payment of dividends by Oracle and SAP, while Workday continues to invest in growth (but does buy back shares). Investors interested in capital gains will be better off with Workday, while income investors should look at the other two companies (their dividend yields are comparable).

Margins and cash flow

When it comes to net margins, Oracle is clearly the leader, while Workday is the laggard, and SAP falls between them:

Surprisingly, the numbers are reversed for free cash flow per share, with Workday ahead of SAP and far ahead of Oracle:

Why has Oracle's FCF declined so much? A scan of its cash flow statement shows a huge increase in its net capital expenditures. It has gone from $6.87 billion in the fiscal year that ended in May 2024 to $21.22 billion in fiscal 2025 and now stands at $48.25 billion on a TTM basis.

At the same time, it is also loading up on long-term debt. That has risen from $76.26 billion in fiscal 2024 to $124.72 billion on a TTM basis. In both the capex and debt cases, it has invested heavily in AI research and development.

Comparing the three firms, Workday has an edge-for now, at least. Its FCF per share means it has more capital available for acquisitions and organic growth, leading to higher returns for shareholders. However, if Oracle's big investments pay off, it could become the undisputed leader in terms of margins and cash flow.

Returns on total capital

Return on total capital provides an overview of what shareholders are getting out of their capital contributions and the debt taken on by management:

Two contrasting scenarios have unfolded over the past several years. First, Workday and SAP are increasing their returns, and second, Oracle has been sliding down.

For Workday and SAP, artificial intelligence is driving new opportunities, as their customers seek to reduce costs, offset a shrinking workforce, and more. New technologies to achieve these challenges are emerging, and their customers want to take advantage of them. If they do not, they may suffer competitive disadvantages.

Oracle, too, has enjoyed growth for the same reason. And, as we saw, it has pushed up its capex and debt even more.

SAP is the best performer here, with Workday not far behind. Oracle is heading in the wrong direction-for now. Again, we point to Oracle's potential.

Valuations

Of course, investors will want to compare the valuations to determine how much it will cost them for one dollar of earnings. The following table provides two conventional measures and two PEG valuations, the latter to capture the value of earnings growth:

Note the very low PEG TTM ratio for SAP; that reflects the firm's rapid earnings growth in the past year. In Q4-2024, it earned $2.78 per share, and by Q4-2025, it had grown EPS to $7.22 per share, which is extraordinary growth. Since PEG FWD is more than 10 times higher, analysts expect that rate of growth to get back to normal going forward.

Workday's high PEG TTM ratio reflects the opposite of SAP's, with slow growth in the past year, from $1.98 to $2.61. However, its PEG FWD is much lower and better, with analysts expecting stronger growth this year.

All three stocks are better values now than they were in the second half of 2025, as their share prices slid, almost in unison:

EPS forecasts

Let's now look at the specific EPS estimates provided by Wall Street analysts (note the variations with the growth table figures above):

A couple of notes: first, the three firms have different year-ending dates, hence the Year 1, etc. Second, Workday is covered by 41 analysts, SAP by 8, and Oracle by 37. All three have fewer analysts as the forecasts go out further.

What do the results suggest? Earlier in this article, it appeared Workday was the growth stock to beat. Now, and with these data points, the growth lead may actually belong to Oracle. Its average annual EPS growth is expected to be roughly 50% higher than those of SAP and Workday, as its heavy capital and debt investments kick in and raise its fundamentals. Curiously, though, the analysts expect growth of only 7.00% in what I'm calling Year 2.

As we will see next, the companies have mostly beaten estimates in the past year.

Quality of earnings

Workday has consistently beaten estimates over the past four quarters. In the most recent quarter, analysts expected revenue of $2.52 billion, while the company delivered $2.53 billion. Similarly, on EPS, analysts forecast $2.32 per share, while the actual number was $2.47.

Oracle beat both revenue and earnings estimates in its latest quarter, Q3-fiscal-year-2026. In Q2, it beat on earnings and missed on revenue. Q1 saw double misses, while Q4-2025 produced beats on both revenue and EPS.

For SAP, Q4-2025 earnings topped estimates, while it missed on revenue. It was the same mix for Q3, Q2, and Q1.

Give Workday a slight lead in this category, with its consistent overperformance, a performance that should help maintain analysts' optimism about future results.

"Should" is an appropriate verb here. Of the 40 Workday revisions posted by Wall Street analysts, 10 are ups and 30 are downs. SAP, on the other hand, has six Ups and one down. Oracle has 34 ups and three downs. Analysts have far greater confidence in Oracle than in Workday and SAP, it seems.

Despite the revision numbers above, when it comes to ratings, Workday has 19 Strong Buys, seven Buys and 17 Holds.

For SAP, the analysts have nine Strong Buys, four Buys, and three Holds.

Oracle comes in with the best ratings: 28 Strong Buys, seven Buys, eight Holds, and one Strong Sell. Again, the edge goes to Oracle.

Turning to returns on total capital [ROTC], SAP has taken the lead from Oracle, while Workday lags them both:

Momentum

With the share price of all three firms declining since last October, there is no positive momentum.

Given that all fell about the same amount over about the same period, we should look to industry headwinds. That includes an increase in interest rates last fall, and increases in the cost of borrowing likely reduced demand among their customers.

The shareholder exit began about the same time as Oracle started boosting its capex and debt. Specifically, in the quarter that ended on November 30, 2025, Oracle boosted its capex by nearly 50%, from $8.50 billion to $12.03 billion. And in the fourth quarter, it upped its capex again by over 50%, from $12.03 billion to $18.64 billion. Investors in SAP and Workday likely asked themselves if their companies would need to make similar commitments.

And, there was other news that kept pressure on prices. In its Q4 fiscal 2026 first quarter earnings report, Workday reported subscription sales had slipped, albeit modestly.

Investors likely were concerned, too, by the increasing power of AI and what it might mean for HCM and ERP workflows. Would AI providers eat into existing business relationships? Could there be major disruptions? Would it drive down prices or margins?

The three firms are integrating AI into their processes and systems, but would that be enough to maintain their competitive edges?

Ratings

Despite these concerns, I believe the outlook for all three stocks is positive. They have well established positions in their industry, with most of big business as their customers. Incumbency is not a moat, but it is a solid advantage and provides time to adapt to the innovations of other firms.

As we saw in the EPS estimates, the analysts expect earnings to grow through at least the next three years. For Workday and SAP, we're looking at average expected returns of 14% to 15%, while Oracle is expected to post average returns of nearly 22%.

Looking specifically at Workday. I give it a Buy rating, based on its history of growing ROTC and beating estimates. And while investors may be concerned about HCM and ERP at the company, CEO Aneel Bhusri said on the Q4-2026 earnings call,

"You've all heard the narrative out there that HR and ERP will be replaced or relegated to the background by AI. I personally just don't see that happening. Our application domains are really, really hard to build. I've been working in the HR and ERP space for over 30 years. These are true systems of record that must process transactions with absolute accuracy and speed, enforce complex security models and comply with statutory and regulatory requirements all over the world. That kind of complexity is very hard to replicate. No amount of vibe coding is going to produce an HR or an ERP system."

Investors interested in Workday might also take a closer look at Oracle, which is expecting faster EPS growth because of its major capex and debt investments.

Interestingly, both Workday and Oracle are expected to keep growing their bottom lines, while their share prices show no sign of recovery. If this divergence continues to expand, there may be value investing opportunities in the near future.

Risk factors

The Seeking Alpha system has placed a warning label across the Workday summary page, warning the firm is at high risk of performing badly. It points specifically to decelerating momentum and negative EPS revisions. In my previous analyses of these warnings, I have found they depend too much on past and current data, and do not factor in forward potential. Nevertheless, investors should consider the warning, and investors considering Oracle and SAP should do the same.

The CEO of Workday considers the threat of AI disruption relatively low, but since we are in the early stages of AI development, investors should consider this threat. No one yet knows where the limits, or even the boundaries, lie.

In a highly competitive tech industry, it is essential to keep up with the leaders and even take the lead at least part of the time. All three companies face this challenge, but it may be more of a burden for Workday, which is smaller than its two rivals by market caps.

Securing customers data, which in the HCM and ERP areas is highly sensitive, is also essential. Any breach of data could lead to reputational and/or financial harm.

All three firms operate internationally, which brings in new exposures and risks. Compliance with regulations and legislation becomes more difficult with each new country they enter. From another perspective, foreign expansion also produces currency exchange risks.

Conclusion

I have confidence Workday will continue to deliver capital gains to its shareholders, along with modest gains through share buybacks. That is the main reason for my Buy rating. Based on the EPS estimates, I expect much the same from SAP, although shareholders in it will receive dividends as well.

Oracle, however, may have the greatest growth potential. The EPS estimates indicate strong capital gains as its investments show up in higher revenue and earnings.

data.title: Molina Healthcare: A Cyclical Long-Term Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Molina Healthcare (MOH) seems misunderstood after a temporary disruption in earnings, making now an attractive entry point for long-term investors. The severe Q4 underperformance appears to be driven by both Medicaid managed care's cyclical nature and retroactive rate adjustments, rather than fundamental problems in the business model.

In the past, Medicaid managed care has operated within a predictable cycle of cost increases that exceed state reimbursement rates. These ultimately return to normal after rates are adjusted. Therefore, it is likely that some form of adjustment in California rates will appear to help reduce some of the burden on Molina's profit margin. Marketplace prices have also increased since the start of 2026, and the Medicare Advantage Part D (MAPD) segment will be exited by 2027, further suggesting that the current downturn is temporary.

Lastly, the current outlook of $5.00 in EPS for 2026 does not seem to reflect normalized earnings potential. This includes ~$2.50 in one-time costs associated with ramping up in Florida and the exit of the Medicare Advantage business. If we were to remove these estimated costs, it is likely that Molina could earn ~$7.50 in normalized EPS. Furthermore, management has indicated that their "embedded earnings" model estimates the company's EPS could reach over $11 in the long term. This is based upon its growth pipeline of awarded government-backed contracts transitioning from investment phase to profitability in the next 12-24 months.

Given these developments, I believe current estimates aren't fully taking into account the recovery potential, contract maturation, or the removal of margin headwinds. This leads me to rate the stock a Buy with a 12-month price target of $188.60, where I expect this recovery to boost estimates as results materialize.

The Q4 Miss

The market's response to Q4 2025 was severe, with the stock dropping ~25% the day after reporting an adjusted EPS of -$2.75, down from $5.05 YoY. Molina noted that its Q4 results were weighed down by around $2.00 per share of unfavorable premium retroactive adjustments in its California Medicaid business. These are revenue items where the state retrospectively adjusted reimbursement, which is a common feature of Medicaid managed care that can often create earnings surprises. The Medicaid MCR for Q4 printed at 93.5%, elevated by cost pressure and timing mismatches, and the Medicare MCR hit 97.5%, driven by high-acuity utilization and the weak MAPD product. The Marketplace MCR was elevated at 92.5%.

With California retroactive adjustments reversing over time as rates catch up, it suggests that these pressures are temporary. The MAPD product, the single biggest negative Medicare item, is exiting entirely for 2027. Marketplace MCR is expected to normalize as rate increases averaging ~30% in 2026 work their way through the book. This is a common pattern in the Medicaid industry margin cycle, and with Molina's stock heavily impacted by these short-term headwinds, it creates an opportunity to buy at the trough as the industry normalizes.

Medicaid Rate Restoration

The managed care Medicaid space moves through a multiyear cycle of growth and decline. Each year the states adjust capitation rates based on predictions of what the medical services will cost. As long as the medical services provided are higher than the actuarially predicted costs (as was the case from 2024 to 2025), the managed care providers take the losses and lobby states for rate corrections. Once the state agrees to an increase (usually within 1-2 years after the initial loss), it allows the providers to make up for their losses by increasing their margins. This has happened multiple times with periods such as 2008-2010 (as unemployment rose), 2015-2017 (millions of new members due to the ACA), and currently 2024-2026. There's evidence that we are now at the trough, with Molina's CEO stating:

We believe our 2026 forecast for Medicaid is the trough for managed Medicaid margins. In this margin trough, we expect that Molina Medicaid will produce a low single-digit margin, not losses, and that the market is underfunded by 300 to 400 basis points.

Every time this has occurred, the managed care companies swung to profit and brought strong per-share gains with them within the next 12-24 months.

The $2.50 EPS Burden

The $5.00 in 2026 adjusted EPS guidance includes $2.50 in burdens explicitly stated by management. The first is a non-recurring $1.50 EPS burden related to the start-up costs associated with the new Florida CMS Medicaid contract. While it will take some time before the contract is profitable, it is estimated that the Florida contract will generate $6 billion in premium revenue yearly once operational. While it does pose short-term headwinds, it makes the long-term story much more rewarding now that those headwinds appear to have been priced in by the market.

The second element is a $1.00 EPS burden, which represents the underperformance of MAPD, which is being discontinued by 2027. At its current scale and composition of members, this product is structurally unprofitable. In 2027, this $1.00 EPS headwind will be $0, placing the company in a better position for long-term growth. When adding these two elements back to guidance, the normalized business is generating ~$7.50 in EPS for 2026. This estimate places them closer to past performance of $11.03 adjusted EPS in 2025, despite having a more unfavorable environment. With a more than 50% drop over the last year, the market appears to have overestimated the short-term headwinds given a likely return to strong earnings.

The $11+ Embedded Earnings

Management said that as a result of newly embedded earnings, the company now expects to earn more than $11 EPS in future years. This indicates the earnings power those contracts could contribute once they reach target margins. One contract alone, the Florida CMS contract, includes a $6 billion premium annually at Molina's historical Medicaid profit margin. These are not speculative contracts and have already been funded by governmental agencies. As the contracts mature over the following years, they move from the embedded earnings to tangible results. When combined with the restored rates on the rest of the current Medicaid book and removal of MAPD, the FY 2027 EPS comes into focus. With estimates of $8.20 EPS and a stock price at ~$150 per share, Molina is trading at about 18x FY 2027 earnings. This appears quite cheap for a managed care company at the beginning of a potential rate restoration cycle.

Upcoming Earnings

Molina reports its Q1 2026 earnings on April 22, 2026, with adjusted EPS estimates of $1.94. This number represents an expected decline from $6.08 YoY, which is already included in guidance. While Q1 has historically been the weakest season, Q1 2026 also absorbs the start-up costs associated with the Florida CMS contract and MAPD drag. Instead of looking at EPS, I'll be looking at other metrics to confirm rate progress.

The first is Medicaid MCR. The company's guidance calls for a rate of 92.9% in 2026, which is elevated over historical norms of 88-89%. A Q1 Medicaid MCR below 93.5%, combined with comments indicating cost trends are stabilizing or moderating, would represent the first signal that the trough is forming. On the other hand, a Q1 Medicaid MCR above 94% would be a negative indicator that the rate-cost imbalance has worsened prior to improving.

Any language regarding Medicaid rate restoration is also another thing to look out for. Specifically, management stated that the broad Medicaid market requires rates to exceed trend by 200-300 basis points to achieve program equilibrium. Any commentary on state rate filings, particularly California, Texas, and Ohio (Molina’s largest markets), would help determine how quickly the trough converts to recovery.

Finally, I will be watching the Florida contract ramp progress. Any update on member enrollment pace, operational build-out, or claims experience in Florida would help investors assess when the burden turns into contribution. Overall, positive developments on these fronts (which I expect) could help increase the potential for a re-rating of the stock's valuation as the market realizes the recovery is beginning.

Valuation

A price per share of ~$150 puts Molina at about 30x FY 2026 forecasted EPS. That seems a little pricey until we remove the temporary burden of $2.50 from our 2026 forecasted EPS. After removing this, the company would be trading at 20x normalized 2026 EPS. By FY 2027, the consensus estimate for EPS is $8.20, putting the stock at ~18x FY 2027 EPS currently. For my 12-month price target, I'm applying a 23x multiple, bringing the price per share to $188.60. This reflects a premium as Molina's story changes from a troubled provider to a diversified growth story, powered by interest rate normalization, the exit of MAPD losses, and the ramp-up of the Florida contracts.

Risks

The greatest risk is how long it will take for states to normalize Medicaid rates. If the major states are unable to do so in the coming years, then EPS would be negatively impacted and affect earnings.

Florida contract execution is the second risk. This is the largest contract in Molina's pipeline and represents $6 billion in annual premium with the state. Implementation costs of this contract also put an additional $1.50 EPS burden on the company. As a result, if the Florida startup slows down, guidance may miss expectations.

Conclusion

The response to Molina's earnings reset has caused the company to be valued based on short-term depressed profitability instead of its sustainable earnings potential. With better earnings visibility because of normalizing Medicaid rates, scaling in Florida, and the exit of Medicare Advantage losses by 2027, there will likely be greater clarity for earnings through 2027. This provides support for re-expansion of multiples, which is why I'm rating the stock a Buy with a 12-month price target of $188.60.

data.title: Retail Sales Jump 1.7% In March, Higher Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Advance Retail Sales Report from the Census Bureau showed consumer spending was more robust than expected in March. Headline sales jumped 1.7%, a sharp acceleration from February's 0.7% rise and above the projected 1.4% growth. This represents the largest monthly increase since January 2023.

The "Gas Price" Factor

While the 1.7% headline jump suggests a consumer boom, the data is heavily skewed by energy costs. Following the escalation of the war with Iran, gas prices surged 15.5% from the previous month, a record high. Because the retail report is not adjusted for price changes, a significant portion of this "growth" reflects the increased cost of filling a tank rather than increased consumer volume.

Here is the introduction from today's report:

Advance Estimates of U.S. Retail and Food ServicesAdvance estimates of U.S. retail and food services sales for March 2026, adjusted for seasonal variation and holiday and trading-day differences, but not for price changes, were $752.1 billion, up 1.7 percent (±0.4 percent) from the previous month, and up 4.0 percent (±0.5 percent) from March 2025. Total sales for the January 2026 through March 2026 period were up 3.7 percent (±0.4 percent) from the same period a year ago. The January 2026 to February 2026 percent change was revised from up 0.6 percent (±0.4 percent) to up 0.7 percent (±0.2 percent).

Retail trade sales were up 1.9 percent (±0.5 percent) from February 2026, and up 4.2 percent (±0.5 percent) from last year. Nonstore retailers were up 10.1 percent (±1.8 percent) from last year, while food services and drinking places were up 2.4 percent (±1.9 percent) from March 2025.

The chart below is a log-scale snapshot of retail sales since the early 1990s. The three exponential regressions through the data help us to evaluate the long-term trend of this key economic indicator.

  1. The light purple line is a linear regression through the complete data series.
  2. The green line is a regression from the start of the series through the end of 2007 and then extrapolated to the present - thus excluding the Financial Crisis.
  3. The blue line is a regression from the start of the series through the end of 2019 and then extrapolated to the present - thus excluding the COVID-19 pandemic.

Monthly retail sales have been above the light purple and blue line since March 2021, signaling increased consumer spending that was most likely pent up as a result of the pandemic.

The year-over-year percent change provides another perspective on the historical trend. Current retail sales are up 4.0% compared to one year ago. Here is the headline series with a callout to the most recent 12 months.

Core Retail Sales (Excluding Autos)

Core sales rose 1.9% in March, outperforming the expected 1.4%. However, because this metric still includes gasoline stations, it remains heavily influenced by the aforementioned energy price spike.

Core sales are up 5.5% year-over-year, the largest annual increase since early 2023.

Retail Sales: “Control” Purchases

To find the true signal through the noise of the energy crisis, we look at "Control" purchases, which is an even more "core" view of retail sales. This series is the most reliable reading of the economy as it excludes motor vehicles & parts, gasoline stations, building materials, food services & drinking places.

Retail sales control purchases rose 0.8% in March. While this beat expectations of 0.2%, the gap between the 1.7% headline figure and the 0.8% control figure highlights how much the Iran-related gas spike inflated the primary numbers.

Similar to the retail sales snapshot chart earlier, the chart below is a log-scale snapshot of control purchases since the early 1990s and includes two of the exponential regressions previously mentioned.

Current control purchases are up 4.8% compared to one year ago.

As shown in the chart below, the "control" series offers a much smoother, less volatile view of consumer health compared to the headline data, which is currently being whipped around by geopolitical instability and energy markets. Note that the two series follow each other closely, but headline sales have more extreme highs and lows than the control series.

Retail sales will impact interest in the SPDR S&P Retail ETF (XRT), VanEck Retail ETF (RTH), Amplify Online Retail ETF (IBUY), and ProShares Online Retail ETF (ONLN).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Macy's: Valuation Has Limited Upside With Technical And Macroeconomic Risks
data.description:
Two months after my previous analysis, we have seen how Macy’s, Inc. (M), hit its four-month low of $16.90. And despite the rebound attempts of the stock price, technical risks are still present. I can’t blame the market considering the increased macroeconomic risks that may weaken its performance further. Valuation is still quite cheap, while fundamentals are still robust. However, these don’t seem enough to justify upside.

Q4 2025: Challenges Persist, But It’s Surviving

As we all know, retailers are among the first ones to experience the direct impact of macroeconomic volatility amid stubborn inflation and tariff wars. Softer consumer spending and weaker purchasing power continue to hammer growth opportunities in this industry. Even an established name like Macy’s, Inc. could not avoid the situation despite having a strategic target market and pricing. Yet, its structural and operational efficiency allow it to stay afloat. This was evident in its most recent report.

In Q4 2025, its operating revenue amounted to $7.9B, down by 1.1% YoY from $8.0B. This reflected the mounting impact of stubborn inflation that continued to squeeze M’s pricing flexibility and consumer spending. This YoY decrease was also weaker than in my previous coverage, with a YoY decrease of only 0.2%. This happened despite the Fed’s efforts to boost economic activity and offset the impact of inflation.

On a lighter note, other revenue rose by 17% to $277M versus $239M YoY. Both components increased, which showed that the policy easing cycle was not totally futile. Credit card revenues net rose by $30M. M’s client base was also still solid as marketing and advertising-related revenues increased.

Meanwhile, COGS and SG&A expenses decreased, which can tell us that the decrease in revenue was also due to M’s decision to lower its capacity or volume. That way, it can avoid unnecessary expenses amid the rising prices. While they were not enough to completely offset the decrease in revenue, they helped M maintain positive profits. In the end, the operating margin remained decent at 7.1% despite decreasing from 7.9% YoY. Note that I did not include disposal, impairment, and interchange fees since they are exceptional expenses and not typical parts of the core business.

Risk Factors

As a known retailer, Macy’s is highly susceptible to risks associated with macroeconomic volatility. You can see how its net sales decreased despite its established reputation and wide domestic market coverage. Now, inflation is still above the 2.0% target, showing that the Fed is still having quite a hard time keeping it under control. After all, positive inflation means that prices are still increasing.

This may be fueled by the impact of the recent Middle East tension. Although the Iran and US ceasefire is still in effect, the skyrocketing oil prices are still evident. This will most likely raise fuel prices, which can increase M’s cost pressures, especially those related to its supply chain and transportation. More importantly, higher oil and fuel prices will raise the expenses in asset-heavy and large sectors like shipping, logistics, manufacturing, and industrial. And because many industries depend on their services, the negative spillovers will extend to them. So, prices may increase further. This can squeeze the budget of many individuals and households, so M’s pricing power may also weaken. Growth opportunities may also thin.

Mitigants

Amid all these headwinds, I believe that M has some strengths and positive aspects to mitigate risks. Again, the phrase is "mitigate risks." I’d like to highlight that this section will highlight risk mitigants, not growth drivers. In the current market environment, I believe that M has limited sales and earnings growth drivers. But at least, I believe it will protect its operations.

One aspect to consider is its business model and pricing structure. Basically, M does not compete with cheaper retailers like Walmart (WMT) and Amazon (AMZN). Its premium products and pricing strategies mainly target middle- and upper-income households, not too rich but not too sensitive to inflation. With that, their spending is more resilient than the customers in the lower-income brackets. This may be the reason its sales decrease remains manageable. The only problem I see here is that customers may not increase their spending so as to please Macy’s. They may either keep their spending steady or reduce it a bit but not totally leave it. That’s just it, but at least its brand reputation ensures a loyal customer base.

Another is that the recent policy easing cycle may support spending using credit cards. This means lower borrowing costs for consumers. With that, using card payments may become crucial in ensuring liquidity among individuals and households. On top of that, M may receive credit card revenues from its bank partners and credit card issuers.

Lastly, robust liquidity ensures its longevity and sustainability. Looking at its balance sheet, M maintains adequate cash and cash equivalents of $1.2B, which is high enough to ensure enough reserves even if it incurs net losses or net cash outflows. Also, it has zero debt maturing within the year. The whole amount has a maturity of more than one year, and cash can cover half of the total. To be more specific, 99% of the total has a maturity of four years and longer. This is in line with the net debt/EBITDA of only 0.8x, which means that M only needs 0.8 years theoretically to repay the whole amount even if it doesn’t have to. Hence, we may prove that it has an ample buffer to sustain its operating capacity and dividends.

Valuation

To reassess the valuation, I decided to update its Dividend Discount Model, or DDM. Its annualized dividend is now $0.77, so its dividend growth rate is also higher at 10.60%. Meanwhile, I still set its risk premium at 6.0% to be consistent with my previous coverage. It is also more conservative than the market standard of 5.0%. But note that the risk-free rate increased in line with the 10-year treasury yield. This has some downward effect on valuation. Lastly, Beta increased to 1.54, which means that M has become more volatile than in my previous coverage. With all this information, my new TP, or target price, is $25.08, which shows that M is cheap with some upside potential. This justifies the slight price rebound from the most recent low.

Meanwhile, its P/E of only 8.41x shows that M is only trading at that multiple relative to its EPS. However, note that EPS growth is not that explosive at only 5.84%. Using this, the PEG is 1.43x (8.41/5.87). This means that M is fully priced and trading more than its earnings growth.

Lastly, P/B using book value and P/CPS using cash per share show that M is still trading at a reasonable level and cheaper than the five-year average. My concern is that the gap between the TP and the current price is narrow. So, its price upside may not be explosive. Considering this, I advise investors to stay on the sidelines.

Technicals

M’s technicals show rebound attempts, but bearish signals continue. The price trend is quite above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, showing that the current momentum is stronger than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. This should not be surprising considering the pullback from December to March. However, the 50-day SMA is still decreasing, which suggests that recent market sentiments are still generally pessimistic. So, we still can’t guarantee that the rebound attempt will lead to a bullish breakout. We must note that the price highs and lows have been decreasing in the past three months, so the downtrend may hold.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI are still in line with the price trend, so we cannot detect any bullish divergence yet. The MACD line is increasing above the signal line within positive territory along with the histogram, which implies that buying volume is increasing and outpacing signal volume. However, RSI is approaching the overbought region. And from the way I see it, it will fail to break above the first key resistance level of $22.78 before becoming overbought. With that, the dip and profit-taking that may follow may lead to a further low.

Conclusion

Macy’s, Inc. is experiencing the negative impact of macroeconomic volatility and geopolitical tensions. However, its solid and robust fundamentals assure us of its survivability and dividend longevity. Nonetheless, it is not enough to justify a rating upgrade. Valuation has limited upside, while technicals still show bearish tendencies despite rebound attempts. I’m reiterating my hold rating on this stock.

data.title: Artisan Partners: Tech-Led Passive Managers May Benefit More From Energy Crisis Resolution
data.description:
Artisan Partners Asset Management Inc. (APAM) has a decent amount of skew towards non-consensus strategies, meaning those weighted away from the largest gravity centers in value weighted indices namely the mega-caps and the US markets in general. With quite big AUMs in ex-US strategies and mid-cap, there is less inflection in the benchmark indices with respect to the outcome of the ceasefire and related peace talks around the Iran War, which is an important consideration for the asset management industry dragging on AUMs and a risk to performance. This is because the biggest effect of the Iran War happens to be on cost of capital assumptions, related to the reinflation concerns, as they have an outsized effect on high multiple stocks like the AI-driven megacaps, where lofty expectations backload a lot of value into discount rate-sensitive horizon values. While APAM logged a great idiosyncratic performance for the 2025 thanks to operating leverage driven by 2025 AUM gains and generation of performance fees on some of its AUM, AUM growth has shown a slowdown in March, and with the tech-led recovery in April, though tentative as the Iran War is still a major issue for markets, likely to benefit more passive AM companies more than APAM whose AUM exposure is more value weighted. So directionally, the tech led recovery could be more beneficial to companies other than APAM.

Considerations

While performance was generally good, profit taking and underperformance in some select strategies, mainly those not heavily indexed to the US market's tech-led rise, led to decent amounts of outflow. AUM growth was good enough to drive a solid growth in AUM a little over 10%.

Some departures leading to compensation forfeitures, as well as solid AUM growth on top of good performance fee generation above what was expected drove revenues up 8%, ahead of the operating expense increase of 5% since last year for the FY data. Adjusted operating income was up 12%.

Looking forward, we focus on the AUM distribution in their various strategies.

Some of the outsized exposures that we would consider to be non-consensus and anti-indexed to global value weighted are in US mid-cap (index are mid-cap US indices), ex-US growth (developed market indices ex-US), international value (also developed market indices ex-US) and high income. The other large AUM pools are indexed towards world indices which are expected to feature quite a bit of US exposure. Observing the comparison indices helps us understand the geographic exposure, where ex-US is non-consensus due to the gravity of that massively capitalized market, and within the US ex-large cap as the scale free distribution of market caps means anything that isn't the overwhelming capitalization of large cap is against the grain.

Our point is that all the non-consensus strategies that index away from areas of the global market that have a lot of gravity in value-weighted indices, with the exception of the high income credit strategy, are going to be less fundamentally sensitive to the headlines and development of the trading dynamics around the Iran War. High income and all the other strategies that feature a lot of tech in all likelihood are going to be very sensitive to changes in cost of capital assumptions as a consequence of the war. While it's inevitable to have exposure to the latter types of strategies being a large AM firm, Artisan Partners really has quite a lot of non-consensus, which we posit means that they are less primed for a possible end to the War and related assumptions of disinflation associated with a calming down of the energy crisis.

The different sensitivities to these cost of capital assumptions changes YTD can be seen in the chart below, where tech, with big horizon values is most sensitive.

With the strong recovery in the tech-led markets around speculated developments with respect to the War demonstrate how important cost of capital considerations are for the broad market. For Artisan Partners, we can see how these developments might affect asset managers, where as of March 31st there's been slight negative progress in AUM development since the beginning of the year as a consequence of the market turmoil. Though we think Artisan Partners will participate less in this latest recovery.

Iran War

We don't want to get too much into the details of the Iran War, ceasefire and possible peace talks as the information keeps changing. But the bottom line is that there doesn't quite seem to be a starting point for the round two of peace talks during the ceasefire, namely on the issue of the blockade and whether the Strait is opened or not. There is even talk of the imminent end to the ceasefire not being deferred so there is a strong possibility of renewed military hostilities, although parties were looking for conditions for a second round of peace talks to take place and markets are shrugging off the threat that they mightn't for now. Regardless of what happens, our position regarding Artisan Partners is that they benefit less incrementally from an end to the War, where relaxing reinflation assumptions will fuel tech-led indices ahead of other indices. Even European growth indices, while higher multiple, are going to be much lower multiple than US tech indices particularly after SaaS-pocalypse, where the European tech landscape was much more dependent on SaaS.

In terms of the yield curve, we point out that rate assumptions are much higher than they were 6 months ago in the short and long-term with a full translation upwards. However, markets are beginning to shrug off the possibility of a much longer tail to this war with more recent rate assumptions seeing a reversal of some of the upward translation, with ambivalence to the latest headlines. This is all a consequence of oil price assumptions related to war and Hormuz speculation. Prices below $80 per barrel are expected by September 2026.

Bottom Line

APAM is trading in line with what we'd consider fair long-term AM multiples at just around 10x PE. This is where European AM tends to trade. State Street Corporation (STT), which is more indexed to passive strategies and tech-led recoveries, is trading around 12x. It's quite normal for the passive AM companies to trade at a bit of a premium due to the secular support of the active to passive shift, as it is generally difficult for active managers to justify their value add. But in terms of directionality, we think that on the possibility of the Iran War becoming resolved quite soon favors passive AM more than active like Artisan Partners for quarters following the one of the coming season, with results to be reported March-ended. Though, that also means there is more downside volatility to AUM, perhaps also more sensitivity to panicked retail flows, in the case of passive strategies, so it's not a free lunch. But assuming peace talks do take place in Islamabad once again, and that a solution is found to the War, we'd consider other picks to be more advantaged.

data.title: O'Reilly Auto Parts: Time To Lean In
data.description:
O'Reilly Automotive, Inc. (ORLY) is one of the highest quality consumer discretionary stocks. The stock remains off its 2025 highs, some 15 points higher from where we are now, but the company is due to report earnings next week, and it could be a chance for shares to start to reverse their slide and, instead, grind higher. I believe that as SG&A leverage can start to come into the fold for the company, it can string together consecutive quarters of earnings beats, leading the stock to have multiple expansions.

The Defensive Auto Aftermarket Powerhouse

For those new to ORLY, the company is the largest public auto aftermarket player. They have business operations on both the DIY and DIFM sides of the project spectrum. DIY simply means a customer walks into one of their retail stores, purchases filters or a new wiper blade or motor oil, and moves on. DIFM is when the professional repairs something on a customer's car. The company operates 6,447 stores as of last quarter across the US. They also have a small presence, albeit growing, in Mexico of 112 stores and 26 stores in Canada. Now, there are trade-offs with these divisions that are important to be aware of.

First, DIY is more economically sensitive, as you might imagine, than DIFM. Overall, about 80% of ORLY's total revenue comes from break/fix, which means if you have a busted windshield, you're going to an O'Reilly store to get it fixed. That skews heavily toward DIFM and less DIY, naturally. That being said, DIY is not all that discretionary, as when your car needs oil or a new wiper blade, you have a certain amount of time before you absolutely need to repair that. They do offer more discretionary products (take air fresheners or steering wheel covers, for example), but these are a small portion of total sales.

The other trade-off to be aware of is that, especially since the post-COVID era of 2021/2022, when consumer wallets were flush, DIY has been slower growing than DIFM. This comes as a margin headwind to the business, as DIFM has more labor costs associated with operating it as a business. Labor costs have become exorbitant and talent tough to find in the industry, so the auto aftermarket companies with the greatest willingness to invest over the last five years have done the best. ORLY, as a result, has become a significant share gainer versus the likes of AutoZone, Inc. (AZO), Genuine Parts Company's (GPC)' NAPA business in the US, and Advance Auto Parts, Inc. (AAP).

This is a highly defensive asset with low drawdowns in sales during periods of economic stress given the high need of purchase by the consumer.

Looking For A Q1 Earnings Beat

ORLY only guides annually. Occasionally, on earnings calls, management may offer color commentary on how to think about the slope of same-store sales growth for the year, but it's generally more of a current month/quarter-to-date type of comment. From a retail sales standpoint, April should largely be tracking to be better than March, but we have to remember prior year laps, especially when it comes to auto aftermarket retail. February and March were quite positive last year, driven by pre-tariff stock-up sales, but April ended up being negative last year as tariffs actually hit. This means that the retail sales data that comes out this week will highlight an easy comparison, and there should be a positive exit rate for management to speak to on the earnings call.

Source: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis

Recall the guide:

  • Net, new store openings 225-235.
  • Same store sales (SSS%) 3-5%.
  • Total revenue $18.7-$19.0 billion.
  • Gross Margin 51.5%-52.0%.
  • Operating Margin 19.2%-19.7%.
  • Tax rate 22.6%.
  • Diluted EPS $3.10-$3.20.
  • Operating cash flow $3.1-$3.5 billion.
  • Capex $1.3-$1.4 billion.
  • Free cash flow $1.8-$2.1 billion.

The problem with ORLY as of late has been inconsistent earnings beats. The company missed five quarters in a row in the trailing two-year period, seen below. Labor costs have been weighing on the SG&A line item, which has caused deleverage. The pressures have not abated for the consumer retail industry broadly, but this is why over the last two years the company has been proactively investing in wages in order to reduce employee turnover and to be competitive versus core peers. As this pressure subsides, earnings beats can grow to be more consistent, and the higher multiple the stock used to command can be restored.

Coming out of last quarter, though, the comments from management were positive. This is from the last call:

"On balance, we view the current sales trends in our DIY business as pretty consistent with what we have seen for the last several quarters now. The comparison is not difficult on DIY from a y/y perspective, as they are simply lapping a positive low-single-digit comp. However, we are pleased to not see any heightened pressure on the consumer that would indicate a more significant negative reaction to economic conditions."

So, if DIY is stable sequentially and the commercial business is more a function of bay volume than anything (higher break/fix mix), then we should be able to underwrite a mid-5% comp in Q1, similar to the 5.6% they posted in Q4. While a comp store sales estimate from the street is not presently available, the street likely modeled the midpoint of their annual guidance at 4%.

Thus, in starting to work through Q1 estimates, I'm modeling SSS% at 5.6% versus street at 4% for revenue of $4.52 billion versus street at $4.46 billion.

Gross profit in the prior year period tracked sales growth at 4% to 51.3%, while SG&A deleveraged at 8% growth in the prior year period. With ORLY having spoken to reduced SG&A investments from the second half of 2025 onward versus the late '24/early '25 cadence, the SG&A spend should start to normalize. The midpoint of their gross margin guidance calls for approximately 40 bps of margin expansion in Q1, so I am modeling gross margin at 51.7%. This should be supported due to cost inflation from tariffs and a stable DIY mix.

Where I believe ORLY can start to carve a positive path for itself is with SG&A. I'm modeling 4% SG&A growth, which drives an operating margin of 19.9% on the quarter, approximately $900 million in operating income dollars. After tax and interest, I arrive at $0.76. There's even wiggle room on the SG&A line item such that should they make proactive investments in the quarter, they can still beat. For example, 5% SG&A growth would result in $0.75 of EPS, and 6% growth would result in $0.73 of EPS. Street is looking for $0.70 in Q1 EPS. Thus, I believe they can beat them.

Since the last time they reported, the Iran War has started and essentially begun to diffuse as tensions are off their worst point, so April spending dynamics towards these last few weeks should largely look encouraging to management versus the prior four weeks.

Valuation - Clear Upside To $107

If the company hits the midpoint of their free cash flow guidance, then it'll be $1.95 billion. This equates to approximately a 2.7% yield at the current stock level. That may not seem like much for this name, but it's rare to get any sort of decent free cash flow yield on this name as it typically trades much tighter.

For FY26/FY27, the street is modeling $3.22/$3.58 in EPS. I am modeling $3.48/$3.86 and expect a guidance raise coming out of Q2. O'Reilly manages their guidance conservatively, so I would not necessarily expect a guidance raise this quarter.

Source: Passage Research

On my EPS estimates, the stock is trading at 26.8x earnings this year. Consumer discretionary best-in-class businesses trade at multiples north of 30x, so I see little reason as to why O'Reilly can't close the gap. The T.J. Maxx Companies (TJX) trade at 33x earnings, and Walmart Inc. (WMT) trades in the low 40s. Consistency with O'Reilly's earnings profile will lead to multiple expansions, as there has not been a positive earnings revision story here for nearly two years.

If the stock can get a low-30s multiple, call it 31x, then at my EPS this year, the price target should be $107, brushing right up against its 2025 high at $108. Eventually, we will get into next year and roll the estimates, and there will be upside towards $120/share, but that's on a longer time frame of about 18 months.

Source: Passage Research

Risks

O'Reilly, like other auto aftermarket companies, is primarily a cost-plus business where they will pass through any cost inflation they get. That cost inflation can actually help to drive sales performance on a comparable store sales basis. Should cost inflation decline or tariffs get rolled back to any meaningful magnitude in the near term, it could put pressure on shares. Labor costs are the next largest risk. SG&A has been rather onerous for O'Reilly, so if the company continues to step up its competitive wage position for store personnel, earnings could disappoint and cause issues for the stock. Another issue is the age of the used car fleet. As Americans own their cars for longer durations, with the current duration being 12.8 years, cars break down more frequently and require more repair. This has been an extending dynamic in recent years, and if it were to start to reverse, it could change a long-standing positive tailwind for the auto aftermarket industry, ORLY included.

Conclusion

O'Reilly's SG&A pressure looks finally set to cool off, which can help restore leverage to the operating profile of the business and drive an earnings revision story higher. For a high-quality business trading 15% off of its 2025 highs, it's a good time to step in. There is clear scope for a low-30s P/E multiple on a forward basis, which produces a price target close to the 2025 high of $108.

data.title: FBND: Higher Quality Fixed-Income Exposure, Monthly Pay With ~4.7% Yield
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

The Fidelity Total Bond ETF (FBND) provides an actively managed approach in an ETF wrapper to provide exposure to a basket of fixed-income securities. Despite being an actively managed fund, the expense ratio is actually on the lower end of the average expense ratio of ETFs across the board. With a lower expense ratio, it can help to deliver slightly better total returns over the long run by investors keeping more of what they earn.

The fund invests fairly heavily in an allocation of risk-free U.S. Treasury Notes but then also incorporates corporate and securitized products. The overall tilt of the fund is a high allocation of quality securities, which means a more modest dividend yield, but that comes with an overall reduced risk of capital losses piling up during tough economic times. On the other hand, it also means greater interest rate sensitivity. So there is always a trade-off to consider in the investing world.

With that said, that could make it more appropriate for a relatively conservative investor.

FBND Basics

  • Dividend Frequency: Monthly
  • Dividend Yield: 4.68% (SEC yield 4.63%)
  • Expense Ratio: 0.36%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $25.03 billion
  • Structure: Active ETF

FBND's investment objective "seeks to provide a high level of current income." In an attempt to achieve this, the fund is actively managed and "normally invests at least 80% of its assets in debt securities of all types and repurchase agreements for those securities."

The expense ratio of 0.36% may seem comparatively high when you consider passively managed ETFs, which often come with razor-thin expense ratios from the large fund sponsors. However, given the active nature of the fund, the 36 basis points is on the lower end—the average expense ratio for the universe of ETFs comes in around 0.50%.

The fund has seen a high demand from investors in the last couple of years or so, with total managed assets more than doubling since our last update.

Performance — Stronger Recent Results

Since our last write-up, the fund has provided positive total returns; however, we also covered the name later in 2024 at a time when the share price was looking like it was ramping up higher. This was initially as yields started to decline on the back of the Fed signaling they would be starting their rate-cutting cycle.

In looking back at the share price chart alone, we will see quite the slide during the 2022/2023 years, when the Fed was hiking rates aggressively and we saw the 10-Year U.S. Treasury surge. As noted at the open, this fund is relatively more conservative, employing a high allocation to high-quality investment-grade fixed-income securities, but it does come with greater overall interest rate sensitivity. The fund's duration comes in at 6.02 years. So this is something worth considering for investors looking at this fund today.

Of course, since the slide, as we've seen from plenty of other fixed-income-focused peers, we have seen stability and some recovery in the share price. With that, we see the last 1- and 3-year annualized performance provided as significantly better than the 5- and 10-year marks. Against the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, FBND has also put up outperformance.

While these certainly aren't necessarily the most exciting returns, the fund isn't designed to provide outsized returns given its more conservative portfolio.

Another comparison that could be appropriate is to benchmark FBND against the passively managed Vanguard Total Bond Market ETF (BND). This fund also provides relatively higher-quality exposure, with a similar average duration of 5.7 years. The fund also has a high allocation to U.S. Government debt.

Given the results since inception, it would appear that FBND's active management has been able to deliver some fairly meaningful higher total returns. This would even be despite BND's razor-thin 0.03% expense ratio, with the higher expense ratio working against FBND.

With that said, we've seen the price under a bit of pressure, and that once again goes back to its relatively longer duration and the rise of risk-free U.S. Treasuries currently. We've seen the 10-Year Treasury stabilize a bit in the last handful of years after its sharp rise in 2022/2023. With the Iran conflict currently pushing the price of oil higher, we are anticipating that inflation will remain quite sticky—above the Fed's 2% target. That should keep the 10-Year Treasury a bit more elevated moving forward, thus keeping pressure on FBND for the moment.

For a longer-term investor, this may not be an issue, but we wouldn't expect too much in the way of share price movement through this year. Barring some sort of shock, I don't necessarily see the 10-Year Treasury rising sharply from this level either, so that can make it worth holding for an investor as well, collecting that monthly dividend.

Distribution — Higher Yields Lead To Higher Payout

Similar to most ETFs, FBND simply pays out the interest received from the underlying portfolio to investors. That means the payout will change from month to month. That includes the last handful of years, as yields rose, which also meant a higher payout from this fund as its underlying income generation started to rise.

This can be reflected in the latest financial reporting over the years. In 2021, the net investment income per share came in at just shy of $1 —we've seen that double now and breach over $2 in the latest years. Therefore, the higher payout that we've seen is reflected in shareholders as well.

At the same time, in fiscal 2021, the fund ended up paying a large distribution from the net realized gains. That resulted in that year seeing a payout of $2.3030 during the 2020 calendar year. This would then drop down to the $1.123 paid in fiscal 2022 before rising quite meaningfully from there in the next few years.

FBND's Portfolio

The yield to worst for the portfolio comes in at 5.14%, with the average weighted coupon at 4.39%. The average weighted market price of the underlying holdings is actually discounted at $94.19, meaning the portfolio, on average, is trading below face value. Worth noting is that this fund carries a massive amount of different positions. As of writing, they have 4518 positions, noting that 4484 of these are long and 34 are short. At the same time, relative to the sheer number of holdings, its top ten holdings remain fairly overweight.

Given the high allocation to U.S. Treasury Notes within the top ten, it is not too surprising that we see AAA allocation of the fund coming in at nearly 70%. Thus comes the relatively longer maturity that the fund holds, with yields on the lower end, resulting in the longer duration figure noted.

The government allocation has seen a slight increase since our last update, which translates into AAA getting a slight uptick as well. This was previously shown to be about 61.65%.

Overall, the broader allocations were more evenly balanced previously between Government, Corporate and securitized, at 33.40%, 33.39%, and 29.23%, respectively. At the same time, the duration came in at 6 years before (now 6.02 years), meaning that we didn't see any significant change on that front.

Another area where they are actively managing the fund is through various derivatives. Besides the significant number of holdings that they provide exposure through, they also have interest rate contracts, forward foreign currency contracts, credit default swaps, and interest rate swaps. This would all be in an effort to hedge the fund to help potentially protect it from losses during certain scenarios.

Conclusion

FBND is a relatively more conservative fixed-income fund that is actively managed. It has been able to exceed the returns of its benchmarks thanks to its greater flexibility, despite having what is a higher expense ratio than BND. On the other hand, the expense ratio is still on the lower end if we compare it against the median expense ratio of all ETFs at 50 basis points. This fund certainly isn't too exciting, but it isn't designed to be either. In the near term, I wouldn't expect to see much in the way of appreciation as inflation remains sticky on the back of higher oil prices. Alternatively, I don't necessarily see the 10-Year Treasury rising out of control either, making it a worthwhile hold candidate for investors looking for its relatively safer but modest yield.

data.title: IWMI: A Different Take On Income Investing
data.description:
NEOS Russell 2000 High Income ETF (IWMI): A Different Take on Income Investing

I rate the NEOS Russell 2000 High Income ETF (IWMI) as a Buy. The main reason is simple. It offers something that’s still hard to find in the options income ETF space: real exposure to small-cap stocks, paired with a strategy that generates income without completely giving up growth potential.

Most high-income ETFs stick to large-cap indexes like the S&P 500 or Nasdaq-100. Many funds end up looking like slight variations of the same idea, especially with mega-cap stocks dominating those indexes with the MAG 7 representing over 30% of the S&P 500. IWMI stands out because it focuses on the Russell 2000, where volatility runs higher and option premiums tend to be richer. That creates a stronger foundation for income while adding exposure to a part of the market that often gets overlooked.

This isn’t only about yield. IWMI gives investors a way to own small caps while getting paid for the added volatility. For anyone trying to boost income without piling even more into mega-cap-heavy strategies, that’s a meaningful difference.

Why IWMI Stands Out

The appeal starts with small caps themselves. They tend to move more, react more to economic shifts, and go through sharper cycles than large caps. In a traditional buy-and-hold portfolio, those traits can feel like a drawback. In an options-based strategy, they can be an advantage.

Higher volatility leads to higher option premiums. That’s the engine behind IWMI’s income. Instead of relying heavily on portfolio turnover or aggressive payout policies, the fund benefits from a stronger underlying environment for generating income.

There’s also a diversification angle. Many investors already have heavy exposure to large-cap stocks, especially in tech. Adding another large-cap income ETF increases yield, but it doesn’t change much else. IWMI adds both income and a different type of market exposure, which helps balance a portfolio.

Russell 2000 top 10 holdings:

The Role of Active Management

IWMI and NEOS funds don't follow a rigid covered call strategy that automatically sells away most of the upside. Instead, NEOS Investments runs an actively managed options strategy where key levers, like how much of the portfolio is overwritten, strike selection, and trade timing, are adjusted continuously. Based on NEOS’s own materials, the fund typically does not maintain a constant 100% overwrite. Coverage often moves within a range (commonly around 50% to 100%) depending on market conditions, which allows part of the portfolio to remain exposed to upside price appreciation as well.

Strike selection is also dynamic. Rather than consistently selling at-the-money calls, NEOS often uses out-of-the-money calls to leave room for price appreciation. In stronger markets, they can push strikes further out and reduce overwrite levels, which lowers immediate income but preserves more upside and protects long term NAV. In flatter or more volatile conditions, they can increase coverage and bring strikes closer to the current price to collect higher premiums and potentially protect from downside price movement.

Timing plays a role as well. Instead of writing options on a fixed monthly schedule, the strategy allows for opportunistic execution, particularly during periods of elevated volatility when premiums are more attractive. This matters even more given IWMI’s exposure to the Russell 2000 Index, where volatility tends to be structurally higher, creating a stronger premium environment to work with.

Looking into the prospectus we see that IWMI's call writing program is not designed to replicate a traditional covered call index outcome. The fund uses index options that are tied to the Russell 2000 and combines this with a clever tax managed structure, which allows the fund to retain more flexibility in how gains are going to be realized and distributed. This matters in total return because it avoids forcing the fund into systematically capping the upside at the same level every options cycle.

This means that IWMI's upside capture is not set in stone, its variable. When overwrite levels are lower and strikes are further out-of-the-money, a huge portion of the portfolio will participate in a rally. This creates a very different return profile compared to a fixed traditional strategy that writes calls on 100% of the underlying. This causes upside to be limited regardless of current market conditions.

That flexibility shows up in outcomes. Traditional Buy-Write covered call funds tend to perform best in flat markets but lag when stocks rise quickly because their upside is capped early. IWMI aims for a middle ground. It collects solid income while still leaving room to benefit from market gains, especially since a portion of the portfolio often remains uncapped.

This is the key point when comparing IWMI to the plain Russell 2000 underlying. A non-income ETF will outperform in a sharp bull market because it will capture 100% of the upside. IWMI is not designed to win in that exact scenario, but it is built to generate a higher total return across multiple market environments. In a range-bound, volatile market, or in markets that are gradually trending higher. In those conditions, the combination of option income and partial upside participation can close the gap and, in some cases, outperform on a total return basis with significantly less BETA and volatility to a portfolio.

The trade-off is clear: by investing in IWMI you will give up some upside in strong rallies in exchange for consistent income and less volatility. The reason to buy IWMI is not that it will always beat the underlying Russell 2000, but it can produce a more balanced outcome when returns are driven as much by volatility and income as price appreciation alone. Most often, IWMI and the Russell 2000 can be bought in pairs, so you can get the best of both worlds.

Why It’s a Buy Right Now

The Buy rating comes down to both opportunity and positioning.

Small caps still look appealing compared to crowded large-cap trades. Valuations have often been more reasonable, and if market leadership starts to broaden, the Russell 2000 has room to move.

At the same time, IWMI doesn’t rely only on price gains. The income component helps carry returns, even if the market moves sideways or climbs slowly. That makes holding the fund easier compared to a pure small-cap ETF, where performance depends heavily on price appreciation.

It also fills a clear role in an income-focused portfolio. This isn’t a core holding, but it works well as way for investors who want higher yield to diversify from big tech.

This is why I believe IWMI's structure aligns with the current setup. If small caps continue to trade with elevated volatility rather than a straight upward trend, IWMI will continue to monetize that volatility and keep turning it into options premiums while capturing some of that upside. This combination increases the probability of competitive total returns versus a passive alternative that would depend on market direction.

Total return comparison between IWMI, SPY, the NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (QQQI), which has a somewhat similar strategy but references QQQ, and the NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI), which has a more flexible options strategy and references the SP500.

Recent total return data shows IWMI outperforming these peers, which reinforces the strength of its structure in the current environment. Compared to large-cap income ETFs like QQQI and SPYI, IWMI benefits from higher small-cap volatility, which supports richer option premiums, and, combined with partial upside participation, has allowed it to outperform across recent periods. Given the current market conditions, where small caps are supported by higher volatility and less crowded positioning, IWMI provides a compelling way to gain exposure while reducing reliance on big tech-heavy portfolios.

The Risks to Watch

Like most high-income ETFs, the biggest concern is NAV (Net asset value) erosion.

If the payouts aren’t supported by the strategy, investors can end up collecting income while the fund’s value slowly declines. That’s something to monitor closely. With IWMI, small-cap volatility adds another layer. It helps generate income, but it also means sharper drawdowns when markets turn.

There’s also the trade-off with upside. Even with active management, an options strategy won’t capture the full benefit of a strong rally. If small caps surge, IWMI should participate, but not to the same extent as a pure index fund.

Finally, the track record is still limited. The idea makes sense, but it hasn’t been tested over a full market cycle yet.

Bottom Line

IWMI brings together three things that are hard to find in one fund: strong income, small-cap exposure, and a flexible options strategy that still leaves room for growth.

It isn’t risk-free, and it shouldn’t replace core equity holdings. Still, for investors looking to increase income while stepping away from the usual large-cap-heavy strategies, IWMI offers a compelling alternative. The same volatility that makes small caps risky also drives the opportunity here.

data.title: SCHD: Why I'm Choosing The S&P 500 Over This Dividend Favorite
data.description:
The Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) is an instrument that ended up becoming a benchmark. I would even say synonymous with dividend investing. It created this status of delivering income — a high quality income.

And of course, there are solid reasons behind this, it is not just hype, after all, SCHD’s track record is quite long. There is really a very interesting methodology behind the ETF, good reasons to invest, and one of them is a solid expense ratio of 0.06%. Not to mention the reasonable 3.4% dividend yield and 14 years of dividend growth, much better than peers (see below).

But at the same time, I do not think it is an obvious buy in the current scenario, especially if we take into account the opportunity cost.

SCHD And The “Forced” Diversification

In my view, we should compare SCHD with the ‘market’, that is, S&P500 (SPY) (IVV) and Nasdaq (QQQ).

This is because even though it is clearly possible to have S&P500, Nasdaq and SCHD in the same portfolio, the investor’s capital is fundamentally a scarce asset, so if you just increased your position in SCHD, it is because you did not increase it in the S&P500 and vice versa.

Considering that most investors nowadays hold passive ETFs that invest in the mentioned indexes, we have a problem of high concentration. High concentration in what? First, there is a very high concentration in technology. More specifically, if you buy SPY today, you are buying 33% in technology (even after the magnificent seven went through a strong correction). But more than that, you are buying more than a third of the portfolio in 10 stocks, such as NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) (GOOG), and Microsoft Corporation (MSFT).

I do not see a problem in investing in these companies, they are excellent compounders, with good prospects and a good valuation. But the problem is that there is a high dependence on these names working out. And that is where SCHD acts as a diversifier that “forces” you to hold stocks that you would not hold, acting almost like a hedge.

A clear example that happened recently was with the energy sector. The two largest positions in SCHD currently are Chevron Corporation (CVX) and Conoco Phillips (COP), two stocks that year to date delivered gains of ~20%. Even though they are the two main stocks, there is no strong dependence on these two names, because their weights are 4.58% and 4.3% respectively, and in the next rebalance, SCHD will sell part of these positions because no stock can exceed 4%.

You might argue that the S&P500 also benefited from this energy trend, and that is true, but while the S&P500 has ~4% of its holdings in energy, SCHD has almost 17%. The part about reducing to 4% also catches my attention because it ends up being a hedge against overvalued stocks. A hypothetical example, if an energy stock that used to trade at 15x obtained a re-rating to 30x, SCHD will benefit, but as soon as the rebalance happens, it will reduce the position at the valuation “peak”. If there is a de-rating afterward, SCHD acted as a hedge. If market optimism really materializes and there is massive cash flow generation, high dividend yield, great, SCHD will also capture part of these gains.

Beyond that, energy is not even the best example in my view. Still talking about sectors, SCHD has 19% in healthcare, 18% in consumer defensive, 8.5% in industrials, 7% in communications, which are sectors that are often ignored or have almost irrelevant weight in most portfolios (including my own), so having this diversification in these sectors ends up being a good hedge when there is a rotation like we saw recently (that’s why it outperformed the S&P500 and Nasdaq YTD), a market less optimistic about growth stocks, and more optimistic with hard assets + huge cash flow companies.

SCHD’s qualitative filter is also very well designed and ends up neutralizing opinions we might have about a stock. For instance, we only have stocks with adequate market cap and volume, after that, only companies that have a reliable dividend (+10 years consecutively), and after that there are still filters for debt, return on equity and dividend growth rate. This ends up making the ETF hold stocks such as Verizon Communications Inc. (VZ), which is a cash cow, because the numbers are good and fit this filter, even if the market is more pessimistic and afraid of disruption.

As I discussed in my recent Verizon article (check it HERE), I would not buy Verizon for the portfolio currently. But 4% as is SCHD’s weight? Ok, it’s acceptable, it is good diversification, SCHD will collect dividends, may capture upside, and if it starts to go wrong (stops paying dividends, has weak ROE and debt metrics), SCHD reduces the position or sells everything.

And the list goes further, at this moment, I would also not like to have a strong position in The Coca-Cola Company (KO) or PepsiCo, Inc. (PEP), but having a little in each just to receive dividends and have this automatic ETF rebalance seems good enough.

It is also worth mentioning that, as I have already said in other articles, I am not an income-oriented investor. But even so, I need to say that there is value in selecting stocks based on dividends, but the relationship is somewhat counterintuitive. I disagree with the idea that a company can be "inherently good" just because it pays dividends. However, it is a truth that great companies are the ones capable of sustaining cash flow and dividends. That is why the filter works. Only companies that are cash flow rich, have controlled debt, can sustain a dividend. More than that, only companies that have moats, such as pricing power, can sustain dividend increases over long periods.

So overall, I think SCHD does play an important role. The expense ratio is low, diversification is high, the methodology is good, and it ends up protecting the investor from concentration risk (for example, having 10% in KO stock just because of the dividend and it suffering a drawdown due to a valuation problem or something like that).

The Reason I Do Not Want To Own SCHD (For Now)

In the long term, as you could see in the section above, I like SCHD, it is a good instrument to compose a portfolio.

But I do not think it is the best moment to buy SCHD.

This is because the positive aspect I mentioned about diversification and “forcing” you to hold several stocks ends up being a double-edged sword. For example, in a moment where the market is dismissing cash cows, does not care about mature companies, and only wants growth, holding SCHD ends up protecting you from a de-rating in tech stocks.

But while this forced diversification acts as a safety net during these corrections, it also creates a significant drag during periods of high-tech growth, sustained tech advancement, or just some major bullish sentiment. Think about it. The S&P500 and Nasdaq right now are fueled with some secular growth stocks, based on AI, cloud computing, and similars. But by mandate, SCHD excludes these high growth stocks because they lack the 10y dividend track record, I know it's fair, but this characteristic limits your upside. I mean, in a market that rewards innovation and scalability (think not only in the next year, but in the next 5), SCHD's heavy tilt towards dividend, value and mature companies, forces you to own these slow growth behemoths.
