But after the de-rating has already happened, which I believe is the case, it seems to make much more sense to be more optimistic with indexes such as the S&P500 and Nasdaq than with an ETF like this. I am not saying it does not make sense to own SCHD, but rather that between buying the index or doing stock picking and buying SCHD, I prefer to buy other things.

I will classify this reason as opportunity cost.

If I think SCHD will not be able to outperform in the next quarters/years, I do not think it makes sense to assign a buy rating to it.

The reason I do not think SCHD will outperform is simple. It is a mix between valuation and growth.

As I mentioned in the previous section, the ETF from Charles Schwab ends up holding many mature companies. Oil companies that already went through a re-rating and are already pricing higher oil prices. And worse, several consumer staples companies that in my view still need to prove they can deliver healthy shareholder value at these prices.

Among the examples, I will mention PepsiCo, The Procter & Gamble Company (PG) and Coca-Cola. Having Verizon, which is a cash cow with a single digit P/E and high dividend yield potential is one thing. Holding these other three companies I mentioned is a different story.

And the reason is that I think all three are a reflection of a market that is mispricing consumer staples. Note that for Coca-Cola, PepsiCo and Procter & Gamble, the consensus forward revenue growth is below low single digits. PG for example, the market expects it to grow 1.9%. Even with slight margin expansion, it is still not possible to be that optimistic about these companies’ bottom line growth in the next year.

This growth would not be a problem at all if the market were pricing these companies as mature consumer staples. But that is not the case. They are pricing these companies with a premium valuation as if they were growing in a healthy way and at an adequate pace. All three companies have a forward P/E above 20x, even with this low growth.

And this is not an exception, several other companies with different profiles also trade at multiples that are not “cheap” and deliver low growth, such as The Hershey Company (HSY) or even Lockheed Martin Corporation (LMT).

Well, as I mentioned, this can also be seen from a positive angle, because even if I disagree, if I allocated 5% of my portfolio to SCHD, I would be forced to hold a small (or tiny) position in each of these companies and consequently be hedged in case the HALO trend continues (heavy assets low obsolescence).

But what cannot be seen as positive is the opportunity cost. Because the ~5% that I would be buying in SCHD, I could be buying other stocks/ETFs that are much more asymmetric. The most obvious is the S&P500.

While SCHD’s profile is, in general, more mature companies that deliver a lot of cash flow but slow growth with a valuation between “fair” and overvalued (of course there are many exceptions), the profile of the S&P500 and Nasdaq are high growth companies with valuations often lower than the companies mentioned.

Just compare the table below with the others. It does not make sense to me to see Microsoft with an estimated growth of 15.6% just for the top line next year, being traded at a P/E practically the same as Coca-Cola. It is also very strange to me to see that Meta has an estimated top line growth above 20%, and the company being literally cheaper than Coca-Cola Company. Of course the risks are different, uncertainties are different, but overall, just as SCHD has the “creme de la creme” of consumer staples, the S&P500 and Nasdaq have the best tech companies.

What I mean by this is that even if uncertainties are there, the asymmetry is even greater. There is an opportunity cost in “passing” on an S&P500 with a very large weight in tech stocks that have very high growth prospects and a valuation well below the average (5y avg forward P/E of MSFT for instance is 31x, a 20% discount) to buy mature companies.

Of course, as I noted in the first section, this does not make SCHD a bad instrument. But I believe these factors need to be analyzed when adding or increasing a SCHD position in the portfolio, the relative valuation versus the main market indexes and this implicit opportunity cost.

Bottom Line Good But I Do Not Want To Buy It Now

I do not see this article as a bearish piece, and I do not think you should see it that way. SCHD’s long-term thesis remains intact, and if I were to give a more specific rating to this ETF, I would say it is a “solid hold”. It operates with a methodology that works as strong diversification and a “bias remover”, buying good companies that pay good dividends even if the market disagrees, but the opposite is also true, at times it will pay a premium for companies that the market is pricing due to some euphoria or a rotation driven by fear in other sectors.

That is what I think is happening now, the market is afraid of a more uncertain scenario in tech, and ends up making traditional SCHD holdings more expensive. Therefore, in the current momentum I prefer to be more selective, either by doing stock picking or preferring to have greater exposure to the S&P500 than to the ETF, which seems to offer a much more attractive and competitive asymmetry.

data.title: Customers Bancorp: As Much As I Want To, I Can't Keep My 'Buy' Rating
data.description:
One of my better calls in recent years has been my bullish stance on Customers Bancorp, Inc. (CUBI). Since I last reaffirmed the company as a "Buy" candidate in early October of last year, the stock has risen 21.6%. That's well above the 5.9% rise that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. But that's not all. I first turned bullish on it in August 2023. And in that time, the stock has skyrocketed 126.6%. The market, meanwhile, is up only 61.4% over that same period. This is a monumental amount of appreciation. And it can be attributed to high-quality assets and continued expansion. At some point, however, all good things must come to an end. And it is my view, based on how the company is priced, that taking a slightly more cautious approach is justified. Because of that, I have decided to downgrade it to a "Hold" for now. Though in all honesty, it wouldn't take much for me to upgrade it once again.

Hopefully, that will happen in the coming days. I say that because, on April 24, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. The current expectation is for revenue and profitability to improve. But unless something really big and unexpected occurs that is a positive for shareholders, I still don't see myself upgrading it. Instead, adopting the aforementioned "Neutral" stance seems to make the most sense.

Time To Scale Back

After such an amazing run higher, it shouldn't be surprising that I am downgrading Customers Bancorp from a "Buy" to a "Hold." This does not mean that I think negatively about the quality of the institution or its operational potential moving forward. In fact, in many respects, the company is doing a fine job. At the end of 2025, for instance, deposits came in at $20.78 billion. That's up from the $18.85 billion that the institution had a year earlier. I am a bit frustrated by the fact that 33% of deposits are brokered. This means that they are high-cost deposits borrowed from other financial institutions. But that alone is not enough to justify a downgrade. I do think it's also worth pointing out that uninsured deposit exposure is quite high. At the end of last year, it amounted to $8.6 billion. That was 41.4% of its overall deposit base. In addition to being above the 30% maximum that I typically like to see, it also happened to be higher than the $7.3 billion, or 38.7% of total deposits, that the company had classified as uninsured in 2024.

As deposits increased, the value of loans grew also. These expanded from $14.31 billion in 2024 to $16.60 billion last year. The most significant chunk of its loan portfolio ended up being the specialized lending category that accounted for $7.09 billion. This includes direct finance and sales-type equipment leases, but it mostly consists of investments in fund finance activities, real estate specialty finance, technology and venture finance, healthcare and financial institutions, and municipal finance. This is inherently riskier than most other forms of exposure. And the size of it means that the risk profile could result in damage should loan performance worsen. I'm not saying that will happen, but it is a risk factor.

We would be wise to talk about other aspects of the balance sheet as well. Take, if you will, the value of securities that the company has. The business ended 2025 with $2.67 billion worth of these assets. That was down from the $3.01 billion that the company had a year earlier. Cash and cash equivalents, meanwhile, went from $3.79 billion up to $4.41 billion. And debt went from $1.41 billion to $1.71 billion. When you combine all of these together, what it means is that the company is ratcheting up leverage some to boost cash and continue to invest heavily in growing its loan portfolio. Obviously, the decision to reduce exposure to securities also played a role here.

As the size and the composition of its balance sheet changed, its overall profitability changed also. In the chart above, you can see that for the final quarter of 2025, we saw a nice jump in net interest income. Normally such a large change would imply some one-time event, like, in the previous year, an outsized provision for credit losses. But that's not what we saw. That metric barely budged, inching up from $21.2 million to $22.3 million. The increase in the size of the balance sheet played a large role, but the company also saw a rise in its net interest margin from 3.11% to 3.40%. In the chart below, you can see that and the change in the yield or costs of some of its balance sheet items. Even though certain assets, such as loans and securities, became less profitable for the company during this time, the company was able to drastically reduce how much it was paying depositors and what it was paying on its debt as the Federal Reserve slashed interest rates. On net, this was a positive for the institution, as the data clearly shows. And the same holds true when it comes to 2025 in its entirety compared to 2024.

Even though a declining interest rate environment has proven beneficial for the institution, this does not mean that we can expect that trend to continue. In fact, if management is correct, declining interest rates could have a negative impact on profitability. In the image below, you can see what rate shocks of between 1% and 3%, both in the positive direction and in the negative direction, would do to net interest income. But the good news is that, even with these items, we are not looking at a huge hit to shareholders if interest rates are cut.

Non-interest income has been quite a bit more volatile. Largely because of substantial losses on the sale of investment securities in the final quarter of 2024, the company saw an increase last year in non-interest income. This was instrumental in pushing net profits up from $23.3 million to $70.1 million. And for the year as a whole, the company reported profits of $209.2 million. That stacks up nicely against the $166.4 million that the business reported the previous year.

In the chart above, you can see how the company is priced in relation to earnings. Unfortunately, with a price-to-earnings multiple of 12.7, the bank is more expensive than I would typically like to see. My preference is for a price-to-earnings multiple of 10 or lower. This also happens to put the institution around the middle of the group compared to the five banks that I stacked it up against. In that same chart, you can see that two of the five firms ended up being cheaper than it is in this regard. That ranking is very similar to the ranking that we get when looking at the price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book multiples. In the chart below, you can see that three of the five firms are cheaper than it is on a price-to-book basis. And two of them are cheaper when it comes to the price-to-tangible-book approach.

Normally, I would have downgraded the company earlier. However, the saving grace for the institution leading up to this point has been its overall asset quality. In the first chart below, you can see a return on assets reading of 1.20%. That's above the 1% minimum that I typically look for, and it puts the company above three of the five banks that I stacked it up against. In the subsequent chart below that, I did the same type of analysis using return on equity. With a reading of 13.28%, our prospect is above the 10% minimum that I typically look for. It also puts the business above four of the five banks that I compared it to.

One last thing I would like to touch on would be credit quality. This is distinct from asset quality. In the image below, you can see certain metrics here. Especially interesting would be non-performing assets and non-accrual loans. These numbers are both quite a bit lower than the 0.50% maximum that I typically like to see. And this means that, at least as of yet, there is not any reason to be materially concerned about the institution.

As I mentioned at the start of this article, on April 24, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, analysts believe that revenue will come in at about $196 million. That would represent a nice jump over the $143 million reported a year earlier. However, this is a bit deceptive. I say that because, during the first quarter of last year, this revenue figure included a $51.3 million impact associated with losses on investment securities. Without this, we would have seen revenue of about $194.3 million. But I digress. Because of that overall weakness, earnings per share last year ended up being just $0.30, which translated to net profits of $9.5 million. But this year, analysts expect profits of $1.89, which translates to $66.9 million.

Takeaway

Based on the data that's available right now, it is my belief that the easy money has been made when it comes to Customers Bancorp. If we were to annualize the financial performance expected for the first quarter of 2026, we would get down to a price-to-earnings multiple of 9.9. But given the other factors that I have already mentioned and the uncertainty about the future, especially in what I believe will be a worsening economic climate, I believe that downgrading it to a respectful "Hold" makes sense here.
